result:
I’ll write about silence — not as an absence, but as a presence.

We think of silence as emptiness, a pause between noises, something to fill. But there’s a kind of silence that is deep and full, like a well that doesn’t echo. It’s the silence of early morning just before the birds begin, when the world is holding its breath. It’s the silence in the middle of a forest, where you can almost hear the trees growing, the roots stretching.

We’re so afraid of it. We stuff our lives with sound — music in our ears, screens flickering, conversations layered over notifications. We mistake noise for aliveness. But silence isn’t dead air. It’s the space where things settle into their true shape. It’s in silence that you can hear your own thoughts, not as a frantic monologue, but as gentle arrivals, like leaves landing on still water.

I remember once sitting alone in an old chapel, not praying, just sitting. The silence there wasn’t hollow; it was dense, centuries of murmured prayers soaked into stone, suspended in the air. I felt it physically, like a soft weight. That’s the thing about real silence — it has texture. You can feel it on your skin.

Then there’s the silence between people. Not the awkward kind, but the comfortable one. Two people reading in the same room, not speaking, yet connected by a quiet thread of understanding. That silence is a language in itself. It says: *I am here with you, and no words are needed.*

But our modern world wages a quiet war against silence. We’ve made it a commodity — “silent retreats,” noise-canceling headphones, soundproof rooms. We pay to get it back. How strange that what was once the default condition of the planet has become a luxury.

Maybe we avoid silence because it asks something of us. In the absence of distraction, we meet ourselves. And that self isn’t always tidy. Silence can hold sadness, regret, loneliness — but it can also hold peace, clarity, and a strange kind of joy. It doesn’t judge. It just contains.

I wonder if the most profound truths aren’t spoken at all, but discovered in silence. Not as revelations that strike like lightning, but as slow realizations that seep up from some deep, quiet place within. The mind, when it stops chasing noise, begins to listen to something else — the hum of existence itself.

So here’s to the silent moments. To staring out the window without a thought. To walking without headphones. To sitting with a cup of tea and just breathing. To the gaps. The pauses. The spaces between.

Because it’s in silence that the world — and we ourselves — can finally be heard.
